summary_text:
Chief Complaint
A hacking cough for about three days now.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports having this hacking cough for about three days now. It started off with a gradual cough and has now progressed to coughing up some green phlegm. He mentions a sore throat and nasal congestion. He's been drinking lots of water and taking over-the-counter cough medication, but it hasn't been helping. He reports being around his sister a few days ago, who has had similar symptoms, and noticed he started to develop a cough shortly after. He denies fever, shortness of breath, body aches, nausea, or vomiting. He also mentions a history of seasonal allergies around spring time, with sneezing and runny nose. He's been trying to avoid salty foods and has had some difficulty with sugar intake. He was on vacation last week and didn't get sick, but he did notice that many people had vacationed last week. He reports that his mother died of pancreatic cancer.

Findings
Physical exam reveals bilateral maxillary sinus tenderness. The patient's lungs are clear to auscultation. A systolic ejection murmur is present at the left base. Blood pressure is elevated at 140/72 mmHg. The patient's blood pressure has been creeping up slightly. The patient reports a cough and nasal congestion.

Assessment
The patient's symptoms are most consistent with an upper respiratory infection, likely viral in origin, given his exposure to a sick family member. He has a cough and sinus tenderness.

Plan
I'm going to start your physical exam. We will check you for COVID. Continue with the cough medicine you've been taking. I recommend coricidin hbp. We will continue you on your metformin five hundred milligrams daily or twice a day. We will schedule your hemoglobin a1c at your next visit. I will increase your hydrochlorothiazide dose to twenty five milligrams once a day. Follow up in four weeks if symptoms persist. Order shoulder radiographs.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Cough.

MEDICAL HISTORY

Patient reports history of seasonal allergies, diabetes type 2, and hypertension.

MEDICATIONS

Patient reports taking metformin 500 mg as well as hydrochlorothiazide every day.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Denies any fever or body aches.
HENT: Reports sore throat and nasal congestion.
Respiratory: Reports productive cough with green phlegm. Denies any shortness of breath.
Gastrointestinal: Denies nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea.

VITALS

Blood Pressure: Elevated at 144/72 mm Hg. The remainder of the vital signs look good.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Head and Face
- Examination: No frontal sinus tenderness. Bilateral maxillary sinus tenderness.

Ears, Nose, Mouth, and Throat
- Examination of Mouth:
- Examination of Throat: Erythema of the pharynx. Tonsils are symmetrical. No exudates.

Neck
- General Examination: Anterior cervical adenopathy.

Respiratory
- Auscultation of Lungs: Clear bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.

Cardiovascular
- Auscultation of Heart: Regular rate and rhythm. 2 out of 6 systolic ejection murmur at the left base.

Musculoskeletal
- Examination: No edema.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Cough.
- Medical Reasoning: The patient presents with symptoms similar to an upper respiratory infection. Most likely it is a virus that is causing her symptoms, especially as she reports being around some else with similar symptoms.
- Patient Education and Counseling: I explained that we often let the virus run its course while we treat her symptoms. I advised her to be cautious with over-the-counter cough medicines as certain ones can affect her blood pressure and diabetes.
- Medical Treatment: She increase her fluid intake. I recommended she try Coricidin HBP as a safer alternative to her cough medicine. The patient should rest frequently. I will order a COVID-19 test.

2. Type 2 diabetes.
- Medical Reasoning: Stable with medication.
- Patient Education and Counseling: I encouraged the patient to continue with dietary restrictions.
- Medical Treatment: We will continue her on her metformin 500 mg twice a day. Repeat hemoglobin A1c will be due at her next visit.

3. Hypertension.
- Medical Reasoning: Her blood pressure is elevated today, and it sounds like it has been trending higher at home as well.
- Patient Education and Counseling: I encouraged the patient to continue limiting her intake of salty foods. >I recommend that she take her hydrochlorothiazide in the morning because it can result in more frequent urination.
- Medical Treatment: We will increase her dose of hydrochlorothiazide to 25 mg once a day. She will continue checking her blood pressure a few times a week at different times of the day.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient's blood pressure has been creeping up slightly.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a longitudinal clinical interpretation/synthesis of BP trends, not a single-visit objective finding. It reflects clinician assessment of an active problem rather than an exam or test result.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports a cough and nasal congestion.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a patient-reported symptom history, not an exam finding or test result, and belongs in the HPI with the rest of the symptom description.

section_miscategorizations.text: I'm going to start your physical exam.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a process statement about performing the exam, not a future therapeutic or diagnostic plan. It is more closely related to the examination process than to the management plan, though its misplacement is unlikely to mislead about clinical content.
